coredata.dc:title: Multi-step strategies for synergistic treatment of urinary tract infections based on D-xylose-decorated antimicrobial peptide carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Chunhui
Zhang, Yajie
Liu, Guowen
Yang, Jianming
Yu, Kaiyuan
Lv, Junqiang
Liu, Ran

Niu, Yuanjie
Wang, Xiaojuan
Wang, Quan
coredata.dc:description: Urinary tract infections (UTIs) caused by Uropathogenic Escherichia coli (UPEC), often reoccur due to the formation of intracellular bacterial colonies (IBCs) and antibiotic resistance. Given the significance of YadC for UPEC infection in our previous study, we developed D-xylose-decorated ɛ-poly-L-lysine (εPL)-based carbon dots (D-xyl@εPLCDs) that can be traced, and employed multi-step approaches to elucidate the functional roles of D-xyl@εPLCDs in UPEC infection. Compared to undecorated particles, D-xyl@εPLCDs demonstrate YadC-dependent bacterial targeting and exhibit enhanced bactericidal activities both intracellularly and extracellularly. Moreover, pre-treatment of D-xyl@εPLCDs before infection blocked the subsequent adhesion and invasion of UPEC to bladder epithelial cells 5637. Increase of ROS production and innate immune responses were observed in bladder epithelial cells 5637 treated with D-xyl@εPLCDs. In addition, treatment of D-xyl@εPLCDs post-infection facilitated clearance of UPEC in the bladders of the UTI mouse model, and reduced ultimate number of neutrophils, macrophages and inflammatory responses raised by invaded bacteria. Collectively, we presented a comprehensive evaluating system to show that D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibits superior bactericidal effects against UPEC, making them a promising candidate for drug development in clinical UTI therapeutics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of D-xyl@εPLCDs. (A) Synthesis Method of ɛPLCDs and D-xyl@εPLCDs. (B) TEM image (above) and size distribution (below) of D-xyl@εPLCDs. Scale bar: 50 nm. (C) FT-IR spectra of ε-poly(L-lysine), Acid red 94, ɛPLCDs, D-xylose and D-xyl@εPLCDs. (D) 1H NMR spectra of ɛPLCDs and D-xyl10%@ɛPLCDs, D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs. (E) XPS full spectrum of D-xyl@εPLCDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (F), N 1s (G), and O 1s (H). (I) XRD of ɛPLCDs (black line) and D-xyl@εPLCDs (red line). (J) UV–vis absorption and (K) Fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of D-xyl@εPLCDs. (L) Fluorescence emission spectra of the D-xyl@εPLCDs excited at different excitation wavelengths (460–510 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

D-xylose-mediated UPEC targeting and anti-bacterial activity of NPs in vitro. (A–B) Confocal analysis of CFT073 and ΔyadC treated with D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs (A). Intensity profile along the white line analysis was shown on the right (B). CFT073 was cultured in the logarithmic phase and incubated with D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs for 30 min. After washing, the treated CFT073 was stained with DAPI and FM4-64 to visualize bacterial DNA and membrane, respectively. Scale bar: 2.5 μm. (C-D) Effects of NPs on the proliferation of CFT073. CFT073 was cultured in 96-well plates and exposed to varying concentrations of NPs for a specified duration. The optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured to assess bacterial growth. The background-subtracted values were shown. (E-F) The live & dead viability/cytotoxicity assay showing the percentage of dead bacteria. NPs-treated bacteria were stained with NucGreen and EthD-III followed by confocal imaging. Scale bar: 5 μm. (G) The SEM images showed damaged CFT073 treated with NPs. The damaged sites on the bacterial surface were indicated by yellow arrows. Scale bar: 200 nm. Data are shown as the mean ± SD, **P < 0.01, one-way ANOVA (F). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The intracellular bacterial targeting NPs in epithelial cells. (A) Confocal images of the NPs uptake in 5637 cells in a time-dependent manner. 5637 cells were incubated with the NPs at concentration of 200 μg/mL for indicated time. The representative confocal images were shown. Scale bar: 10 μm. (B–C) Cytoplasmic localization of NPs in 5637 cells after uptake (B). 5637 cells treated with NPs were stained with lysotracker and DAPI followed by confocal imaging. The Pearson's coefficient of the NPs with lysosomal marker was shown on right (C). Scale bar: 10 μm. (D-E) Colocalization of invaded CFT073 with the NPs in 5637 cells (D). 5637 cells were infected by CFT073 for 1 h and treated with the NPs and Gentamycin for additional 3 h. Invaded CFT073 in 5637 cells were stained with anti-LPS plus CoraLite 594 secondary antibody. The nucleus was labeled with DAPI. The colocalization ratio of invaded CFT073 with the NPs was shown on the right (E). Scale bar: 10 μm. (F) Effects of the free D-xylose on NPs-inhibited UPEC proliferation. 105 CFU of CFT073 was cultured in 96-well plates and exposed to D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs (100 μg/mL) with or without addition of the free D-xylose (25 mg/mL) for 12 h. Bacterial growth was assessed per hour by measuring the OD600, and the values were shown after subtracting the background. Data are shown as the mean ± SD, ****P < 0.0001, one-way ANOVA (E), two-tailed unpaired Student's t-test (C).

The antibacterial activity of NPs in epithelial cells post-infection. (A) Schematic illustration of the experimental design testing impact of NPs on bacterial infected cells. For bacterial entry, 5637 cells were infected by CFT073 for half an hour, washed and subsequently treated with NPs and Gentamycin for half an hour. For clearance of intracellular bacteria, 5637 cells were infected with CFT073 for 1 h, washed and then treated with the NPs and gentamycin for additional 3 h. The CFU count was determined by plating the lysate of infected cells on LB agar following washing procedures. To assess the overall impact of the NPs on intracellular and exocellular bacteria, CFT073-infected 5637 cells were treated with the NPs in the absence of gentamycin for 1 h, washed and incubated for additional period of time. Both supernatant and lysate from infected cells were collected for CFU quantification. (B–C) The number of intracellular CFT073 was reduced by post-infection treatment with NPs (B). Confocal images revealed the invaded CFT073 (anti-LPS, blue) in 5637 cells (C). The cytoskeleton was stained using Rhodamine (red). Scale bar: 10 μm. Quantification was shown on the right. (D) The bacterial invasion assay of ΔyadC in 5637 cells treated with NPs. CFT073 and ΔyadC were subjected to infection of 5637 cells followed by the NPs treatments. (E) Effects of the NPs on the entry of CFT073 in 5637 cells. (F) The total effects of the NPs on intracellular and extracellular bacteria were shown. Data are shown as the mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001, one-way ANOVA (B–C, E-F), two-way ANOVA (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The anti-bacterial activity of the NPs in mouse model with acute UTI. (A) Schematics of mouse model with acute UTI and the NPs treatments. (B) The distribution of the NPs in mouse organs. Female mice were transurethrally injected with the NPs for half an hour. The fluorescent signals were detected and quantified by IVIS spectrum in vivo imaging system. n = 5 mice. (C-E) The representative images of LB agar plates inoculated with bladder homogenate from infected mice following NPs treatments (C). The control group's bacterial mixture was diluted by 1000-fold, while the ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@εPLCDs groups' mixtures were diluted by 100-fold. The bacterial mixture in the D-xyl20%@εPLCDs group remained undiluted. The quantification of LB agar plates was shown (D). Female mice were anesthetized and transurethrally injected with NPs for 6 h and then treated with NPs overnight. n = 4 mice. The H&E staining of bladder sections were shown (E). Scale bar: 200 μm. (F) Flow cytometry analysis of CD45+, neutrophils, macrophages and NK cells in bladders of NPs-treated CFT073-infected mice. Data are shown as the mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001, one-way ANOVA (D and F), two-tailed unpaired Student's t-test (B).

Effects of the NPs on gene expression in infected-mouse bladders. (A) Volcano plots showing the differential expression genes in infected-mouse bladders with the NPs treatments (p
adjust < 0.001, fold change >2). Green, down-regulated genes; red, up-regulated genes; blue, genes with no significant changes. (B) KEGG pathway analysis showing distinct differences in pathways of D-xyl@εPLCDs with control and ɛPLCDs. (C-D) Heatmap of genes enriched in NF-kappa B and TNF signaling pathway. The exact changes are represented as log10-transformed values. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematics of D-xylose-conjugated ɛPLCDs exhibit efficacy in vitro and in vivo against UPEC. Antimicrobial D-xylose-conjugated carbon dots was synthesized using substrates including Acid Red 94, ɛPL and D-xylose for the purpose of eliminating UPEC in several key stages of UPEC infection: I) D-xyl@εPLCDs immediately target and kill free UPEC; II) D-xyl@εPLCDs inhibit UPEC adhesion to cell surface; III) D-xyl@εPLCDs enter bladder epithelial cells, targeting and eradicating intracellular UPEC. Alternatively, the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is increased in infected cells by D-xyl@εPLCDs, creating a hostile environment against the proliferation of UPEC.

head.ce:title: Multi-step strategies for synergistic treatment of urinary tract infections based on D-xylose-decorated antimicrobial peptide carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Urinary tract infections (UTIs) caused by Uropathogenic Escherichia coli (UPEC), often reoccur due to the formation of intracellular bacterial colonies (IBCs) and antibiotic resistance. Given the significance of YadC for UPEC infection in our previous study, we developed D-xylose-decorated ɛ-poly-L-lysine (εPL)-based carbon dots (D-xyl@εPLCDs) that can be traced, and employed multi-step approaches to elucidate the functional roles of D-xyl@εPLCDs in UPEC infection. Compared to undecorated particles, D-xyl@εPLCDs demonstrate YadC-dependent bacterial targeting and exhibit enhanced bactericidal activities both intracellularly and extracellularly. Moreover, pre-treatment of D-xyl@εPLCDs before infection blocked the subsequent adhesion and invasion of UPEC to bladder epithelial cells 5637. Increase of ROS production and innate immune responses were observed in bladder epithelial cells 5637 treated with D-xyl@εPLCDs. In addition, treatment of D-xyl@εPLCDs post-infection facilitated clearance of UPEC in the bladders of the UTI mouse model, and reduced ultimate number of neutrophils, macrophages and inflammatory responses raised by invaded bacteria. Collectively, we presented a comprehensive evaluating system to show that D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibits superior bactericidal effects against UPEC, making them a promising candidate for drug development in clinical UTI therapeutics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are a prevalent and recurrent bacterial infectious disease worldwide, particularly common in bedridden individuals with indwelling catheters, leading to significant medical and financial costs [1]. Uropathogenic Escherichia coli (UPEC) is responsible for over 70% of UTIs due to its ability to colonize the urinary tract and form intracellular bacterial communities (IBCs), which contribute to recurrent UTIs [2].

Due to disrupted microbiota diversity and antibiotic resistance following antibiotic treatments, which normally cause negative health consequences, targeted therapeutic approaches are urgently needed [3,4]. However, eradicating intracellular UPEC from the urinary epithelium through conventional clinical antibiotic therapy encounters several challenges, including limited cell membrane penetration and intracellular accumulation of antibiotics [5], reduced antibiotic activity due to harsh acidic and hydrolytic microenvironments within lysosomes [6], and poor permeability of antibiotics to IBCs [7]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop innovative non-antibiotic antimicrobial agents targeting intracellular UPEC.

Carbon dots-based nanoparticles have been recognized as a promising antimicrobial agent due to their ability to inhibit pathogen adhesion, disrupt bacterial membranes, modulate reactive oxygen species, and penetrate intracellularly [8–12]. Excellent performance has been reported against Helicobacter pylori in gastric epithelial cells [13], Salmonella infection in typhoid and tuberculosis macrophages [14], subcutaneous Staphylococcus aureus-infected abscesses [15,16], and intracellular bacterial infections in tumor cells [17]. Recently, polydopamine nanoparticles (NPs) modified with biphenyl mannoside and a low-temperature photothermal therapy were used to kill UPEC outside of the bladder mucosa and prevent further bacterial invasion [18].

ɛ-poly-L-lysine (εPL) is a cationic amino acid polymer naturally produced by microbial fermentation [19]. It consists of 25–35 L-lysine residues and is widely utilized as an antimicrobial peptide due to its exceptional antibacterial activity, high water solubility, excellent safety profile, and biodegradable properties [20]. Early studies have demonstrated the antibacterial activity of εPL against multiple broad-spectrum bacteria in vitro, including E. coli [21,22], S. aureus, Listeria monocytogenes, Bacillus cereus [23], Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Serratia marcescens [24]. The potential mechanisms underlying the inhibitory effects of εPL on bacterial survival involve disruption of bacterial membranes and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [22]. However, limited antibacterial efficacy of εPL has been demonstrated against intracellular or organ-residing bacteria [25].

Our previous research has demonstrated that YadC, the apical adhesin of UPEC, facilitates the adherence and invasion of UPEC to bladder epithelial cells through receptor binding. D-xylose could bind to YadC and prevent UPEC from interacting with epithelial cells [26]. This study aimed to design a novel antimicrobial peptide-based nanomedicine modified with D-xylose that could reduce UPEC invasion into uroepithelial cells and effectively target and kill UPEC inside or outside of the cells (Scheme 1 ), with the expectation of providing an effective antibacterial agent and treatment strategy for UTIs.

We have previously reported that D-xylose specifically targets YadC, an adhesin found on UPEC, and effectively inhibits its adhesion and invasion into bladder epithelial cells [26]. Therefore, we propose that the D-xylose modification of carbon dots enhances their interaction with UPEC through YadC, particularly within intracellular compartments. The D-xyl@εPLCDs are composed of ɛPLCDs conjugated with D-xylose. In a typical synthesis reaction, the ultrasmall ɛPLCDs were first synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method between ε-poly (L-lysine) and Acid red 94. Subsequently, D-xyl@εPLCDs were fabricated by reductive amination of ɛPLCDs with D-xylose, the targeted ligand, in the presence of NaBH4 (Fig. 1 A).

To understand the physicochemical properties of the as-prepared D-xyl@εPLCDs, we performed several kinds of characterizations. TEM images of D-xyl@εPLCDs revealed that the well-monodispersed D-xyl@εPLCDs had an average particle size of about 1.53 nm (Fig. 1B), slightly larger than the unconjugated original ɛPLCDs (1.45 nm, Fig. S1A). FT-IR spectra of D-xyl@εPLCDs, ɛPLCDs, εPL, D-xylose, and Acid red 94 were compared in Fig. 1C. Our results revealed that the ɛPLCDs carbon dots inherit the characteristic peaks of their carbon source of εPL and acidic red 94: 3420 cm−1 and 3251 cm−1 (O–H and N–H stretching vibration peaks), 2926 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration peaks), 1564 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration peaks), 1500 cm−1 (C–C stretching vibration peak), 1672 cm−1 (CO bending vibration peak), while the CO bending vibration peak was slightly different from Acid red94 (1583 cm−1), because of the π-conjugation increased delocalization of π electrons and decreased bond order of CO bond. In addition, FT-IR (3251 cm−1) showed a large number of –NH2 groups on the surface of carbon dots. The amine density on the carbon dots surface was approximately calculated as 267 ± 27 nmol/mg of ɛPLCDs by Ninhydrin colorimetric assay (Fig. S1G) [27,28], indicating their potential to be used for subsequent functionalization. D-xyl@ɛPLCDs showed a significant enhancement of the C–N stretching vibration absorption peak at 1449 cm−1, suggesting that the Schiff base bonds occurring from the reaction of the aldehyde group of D-xylose with the amino group of εPL were reduced to C–N bonds, indicating that D-xylose was successfully coupled to the surface of the carbon dots [29]. In addition, the vibrational peaks of both O–H and C–H in the spectra of D-xyl@ɛPLCDs were significantly enhanced due to the introduction of D-xylose.

For the 1H NMR spectrum of ɛPL (Fig. S1B), the chemical shift of peak of methylene (a, δ = 3.83 ppm) adjacent with terminal –NH2 was different from the one (aʹ, δ = 3.61 ppm) in the intermediate units. The same results can be found between the signal of the intermediate methine (e, δ = 3.10 ppm) and the one (eʹ, δ = 2.89 ppm) adjacent with terminal –COOH. Therefore, the average degree of polymerization of L-lysine (
n ‾
) can be determined by comparing the integration of the corresponding areas of peak e (or a) to the one of peak eʹ (or aʹ), and the calculated average degree
n ‾
was 34 obtained from the average values from (AeH/AeʹH +1) or (AaH/AaʹH +1) [30]. It can be seen that ɛPLCDs shared the very similar peak profile to those of ɛPL, and proton peaks from ɛPL also exhibited sharp peaks. Tao reported [31]that the ɛPL used here obtained from traditional biosynthetic procedures possessed more sharper proton peaks, while the ones obtained by chemosynthetic process were broaden. To verify the conjugation of D-xylose, further characterization of the carbon dots before and after D-xylosylation were detected by 1H NMR (Fig. 1D). We compared the spectra of ɛPLCDs, D-xyl10%@ɛPLCDs, D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs, results showed that peaks b, c, d (1–2 ppm) of CH2 protons and peak e (3.10 ppm) of CH proton in the spectrum of D-xyl@ɛPLCDs were similar to those in the spectrum of ɛPLCDs, indicating that the ɛPLCDs framework remained after D-xylosylation. Peaks (f, g, h, i, j) appeared in the region of 3.2–3.7 ppm can be contributed to the CH2 proton peak on the D-xylose. As increasing of D-xylosylation, for methine adjacent with –NH2 groups in ɛPLCDs shifted to peak (a) in D-xylosylation unit, all those changes suggested that D-xylose was successfully grafted to the carbon dot surface. It also can be seen that peaks at 1–2 ppm were broadening, which probably attributed to their disordered, amorphous structure [32].

The high-resolution XPS was used to characterize D-xyl@εPLCDs ( Fig. 1E–H) and εPLCDs (Figs. S1B–E). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum showed sp2/sp3 carbons C–C/CC (284.68eV), nitrous carbons C–N/C–O (285.38) eV, and carbonyl carbons CO (287.93 eV) (Fig. 1F). The N 1s spectrum showed C–N–C (401.03eV) and C–NH2 (399.23 eV) (Fig. 1G). The O 1s spectrum showed the presence of CO (530.98 eV) and C–O (532.18eV) (Fig. 1H). XPS results indicated that the surface of D-xyl@εPLCDs was very similar to εPLCDs, but D-xyl@εPLCDs had more abundance of oxygen-containing functionalities and less amine groups, indicating the successful coupling of D-xylose onto the εPLCDs surface. XPS spectra also displayed the presence of C, N and O atoms (Table S2). After D-xylosylation, the increase in grafting of D-xylose groups in D-xyl@ɛPLCDs compared to ɛPLCDs resulted an increase of C/N ratio, and the C/N ratio in the samples of ɛPLCDs, D-xyl10%@ɛPLCDs, D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs was 3.26, 3.41, 3.55 and 3.84, relatively. ɛPL is a homopolymer of L-Lysine, with an average degree of polymerization of L-lysine (
n ‾
= 34) determined by mentioned above 1H NMR. Meanwhile, ɛPLCDs were polymer carbon dots with conjugation of numbers of ɛPL to Acid red by one-step hydrothermal method, which could be denoted as (ɛPL)x(Acid red)m, further as (L-lysine)n(Acid red)m. According to the relative ratios mass of elements determined from XPS (Table S2), there was no nitrogen in Acid red, ɛPL provided 19.38% of N in ɛPLCDs, the roughly estimated degree of L-lysine (DSLLY%) in ɛPLCDs was calculated as 97% using the equation in Table S2 [33], which mean that ɛPLCDs could be denoted as (L-lysine)97(Acid red)3. Further, the average number of ɛPL attached on ɛPLCDs can be obtained from
n
LLy /
n ‾
the
average
degree
of
polymerzation
of
ɛ PL
, and the result was about 3. And the degree of substitution of D-xylose (DS%) in D-xyl10%@ɛPLCDs, D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs was calculated to be about 6.7%, 13% and 26%, respectively [9].

The structures of the carbon dots were analyzed using XRD, and our results showed that the synthesized ɛPLCDs had an amorphous phase structure (22.42° peak), while the position of the 21.95° peak in XRD represents internally disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 1I), indicating that the synthesized D-xyl@εPLCDs remained as amorphous structures.

The optical properties of D-xyl@εPLCDs and ɛPLCDs were analyzed. The prepared ɛPLCDs exhibited very bright green fluorescence emission under UV light (inset in Fig. S1H). Using Rhodamine B with a FLQY of 89% as a standard regent, the relative FLQY of the ɛPLCDs was 80.1%. Similar to ɛPLCDs (Fig. S1H), the UV absorption peak (Fig. 1J) and the maximum excitation wavelength of D-xyl@εPLCDs were located at 511 nm, and its maximum emission wavelength was 530 nm (Fig. 1K). When D-xyl@εPLCDs were excited at different excitation wavelengths from 460 to 510 nm, εPLCDs also showed similar excitation wavelength-independent emission characteristics (Fig. 1L and Fig. S1I). Besides, the fluorescence intensity of D-xyl@εPLCDs under different conditions was analyzed in a series of solutions with different pH values (pH 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4) (Fig. S1J), as well as PBS containing a variety of representative ions, sugars, or proteins to imitate the intracellular species [34]. Our results revealed that D-xyl@εPLCDs could maintain stability in PBS containing a variety of intracellular species (Fig. S1K). However, the fluorescence intensity varied significantly when pH changed, due to changes in surfaces and energy gaps [35] caused by the protonation of the amine-terminated D-xyl@εPLCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: D-xylose mediates UPEC targeting and antibacterial activity of the nanoparticles (NPs) in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the extracellular bacterial targeting efficacy of D-xyl@εPLCDs, we employed confocal microscopy to visualize the common UPEC strain CFT073 and its mutant strain ΔyadC, which were pre-incubated with D-xyl40%@εPLCDs (40% grafting degree) and the bacterial membrane dye FM4-64. Our results revealed that D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibited reduced colocalization with ΔyadC compared with CFT073 (Fig. 2 A and B). Of note, the majority of the signals of D-xyl@εPLCDs were observed on the membrane of CFT073 rather than in its cytoplasmic compartment.

As previously reported, ε-poly-L-lysine has been shown to adhere to and exert toxicity towards the membranes of various bacteria strains, including UPEC. The NPs including ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs were subsequently examined, revealing that the minimum inhibitory (MIC) of ɛPLCDs were 25 μg/mL, while that of D-xyl@εPLCDs was 12.5 μg/mL (Fig. S2A). Both ɛPLCDs and D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibited potent inhibitory effects on the growth of CFT073. The results indicated that the NPs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL could significantly suppress the proliferation of CFT073 during the logarithmic phase after 8 h post-treatment (Fig. 2C and D). Similarly, the colony-forming units (CFUs) of CFT073 released in the cultured media in the presence of 5637 cells (the bladder epithelial cell) were significantly decreased after treatments with the NPs post-infection (Fig. S2B). To confirm the bactericidal effects of the NPs on UPEC, NPs-treated CFT073 was further subjected to a live and dead viability/cytotoxicity assay using EthD-III, a cell membrane impermeable dye. A 3-fold increase in the mortality of CFT073 was found in NPs-treated groups compared to the control group (Fig. 2E and F). The flow cytometry analysis yielded similar results, indicating that the membrane permeability of CFT073 was enhanced following NPs treatment (Fig. S2C). Furthermore, SEM images revealed that the cell membrane of CFT073 exhibited abnormal features such as cracking, wrinkling and shrinking after being incubated with the NPs for 2 h (Fig. 2G). Membrane stripping, abnormal cytoplasmic distribution, and cytoplasmic leakage were also observed in CFT073 released from NPs-treated 5637 cells by TEM (Fig. S2D). The inhibitory activity of the NPs was also observed in cultured drug-resistant strains of UPEC, which exhibited strong resistance to Gentamycin and Norfloxacin (Fig. S2E). Consistent with this, results from the resistant development assay indicated that the engineered NPs failed to induce the emergence of resistant strains after 10 passages, while the MIC of Gentamycin and Norfloxacin increased approximately 98-fold or 1302-fold, respectively (Fig. S2F). Overall, D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibits direct targeting and antibacterial effects against UPEC in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intracellular bacterial targeting and antibacterial activity of the NPs in epithelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike other pathogenic bacteria, UPEC has the ability to survive within host epithelial cells and cause recurrent UTIs. Therefore, it is crucial for the NPs to enter these cells. To evaluate the intracellular activity of the D-xylose-modified NPs, we subsequently investigated their uptake in the bladder epithelial cell 5637. Confocal images indicated a time-dependent increase in NPs uptake (Fig. 3 A). Different from other reports about amine-terminated carbon dots for long-term lysosome imaging [36,37], the endocytosed D-xylose-modified NPs were distributed within the cytoplasm and partially co-localized with lysosomes in 5637 cells (Fig. 3B and C). With an increase in the grafting degree of D-xylose, a greater co-localization of the NPs and the intracellular CFT073 was observed (Fig. 3D and E), indicating that the toxic D-xylose modification increases the ability of the NPs to target the intracellular CFT073. The addition of free D-xylose in NPs-treated CFT073, which may interrupt the binding of the NPs on UPEC with YadC adhesin, significantly reduced the inhibitory effects of the NPs on bacterial proliferation (Fig. 3F). Additionally, we evaluated the biosafety properties of the NPs at varying concentrations on 5637 cells, which exhibited no toxicity after 24- or 48-h NPs treatments (Fig. S3A, left panel). Comparable findings were also noted for HK-2 (normal adult human kidney) and 2BS (normal human embryonic lung fibroblast) cells (Fig. S3A, right panel).

The production of ROS exhibits antibacterial activity in infected cells, particularly in epithelial cells or immune cells [38,39]. The NPs with or without D-xylose modification, could increase ROS production in 5637 cells after a period of treatment (Fig. S3B), providing another potential mechanism for anti-bacterial activities. The effect of D-xyl@εPLCDs on ROS induction seemed more significant in the macrophage cell RAW264.7 (Fig. S3C). Gene sequencing analysis further revealed 1214 up-regulated genes and 650 down-regulated genes in 5637 cells treated with D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs, compared with the control group. A total of 714 genes were found to be up-regulated while 717 genes were down-regulated in the D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs group compared with the ɛPLCDs group (Fig. S3D). The KEGG analysis revealed a significant enrichment of up-regulated genes in various signaling pathways, including TNF, NF-kappa B, JAK-STAT, IL-17, and NOD-like receptor, when comparing the D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs group with the ɛPLCDs or the control group (Fig. S3E), and representative genes in the TNF and NF-kappa B pathways were observed (Figs. S3F–G). These results indicate that D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs treatment of 5637 cells resulted in the induction of genes involved in inflammation signaling pathways, which contribute to the clearance of infected intracellular bacteria.

To determine the roles of the NPs in CFT073 infection of epithelial cells, we systematically characterized their antibacterial activity in bacterial adhesion, entry and intracellular replication (Fig. 4A and Fig. S5A). Results indicated that the number of intracellular bacteria decreased with increasing concentrations of NPs, especially for D-xyl@εPLCDs (Fig. 4 B). Accordingly, confocal images revealed a reduced number of intracellular CFT073 in 5637 cells treated with the NPs (Fig. 4C). Similar findings were also observed in clinical strains of UPEC-infected 5637 cells, as well as in other bladder epithelial cells such as T24 and J82 or the macrophage cell RAW 264.7 (Figs. S4A–D). Deletion of yadC in CFT073 or UTI89 abrogated this significant antibacterial activity of D-xyl@εPLCDs (Fig. 4D and Fig. S4E), indicating that the enhanced intracellular bactericidal effects of D-xyl@εPLCDs depend on D-xylose-mediated targeting. Similarly, non-specific monosaccharides such as D-glucose, as a contrast to confirm the specificity of D-xylose, was decorated on the εPLCDs (D-glu@εPLCDs) (Figs. S8A and S8B). The inhibitory activity against intracellular CFT073 was diminished when treating with D-glu@εPLCDs and the parental εPL, compared to that of D-xyl@εPLCDs (Fig. S4F). Moreover, bacterial entry was suppressed upon post-infection treatment of 5637 cells with D-xyl@εPLCDs (Fig. 4E). The post-treatment of the NPs resulted in a significant reduction in the total number of intracellular and extracellular bacteria within infected cells, thus demonstrating overall beneficial effects (Fig. 4F).

To investigate the roles of NPs in CFT073 adhesion, we pretreated 5637 cells with the NPs before adding CFT073 (Fig. S5A). The results from bacterial adhesion assays indicated that D-xyl@εPLCDs significantly reduced the adhesion of CFT073 on the surface of 5637 cells compared to the ɛPLCDs group (Fig. S5B), which was also confirmed using confocal analysis (Fig. S5C). Subsequently, pre-treatment with D-xyl@εPLCDs resulted in decreased intracellular bacteria compared to the ɛPLCDs group (Fig. S5D). Together, depending on their targeting properties, D-xyl@εPLCDs can inhibit the adhesion and entry of UPEC into bladder cells while also effectively killing intracellular bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antibacterial activity of NPs in the mouse model of acute UTI
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo roles of NPs, we administered transurethral injections of the NPs to mice with acute UTI before or after UPEC infection for therapeutic and preventive purposes, as shown in Fig. 5 A. The distribution of the NPs in mouse organs was determined using a small animal live imaging system at 0.5-, 2-, 4- and 6-h post-injection. Significant fluorescent signals were detected in the bladders of mice after only half an hour post-injection but not in the other tissues, such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung or kidney (Fig. 5B). Strong signals were also detected at the bladder sites of live mice (Fig. S6A). However, these signals disappeared after 2 h of incubation in mice, possibly due to urination and tissue barrier signal blockage. In the mouse model with acute UTI, CFT073 was transurethrally injected for 6 h followed by a 12-h treatment with NPs. The CFU count in the bladders of CFT073-infected mice significantly decreased in groups treated with the NPs, particularly those with 20% grafting of D-xylose, which exhibited the best bactericidal activity (Fig. 5C and D). Confocal imaging verified the reduced presence of intracellular bacteria within the infected mouse bladder following treatment with the NPs (Fig. S6B). However, the NPs had no effect on bacteria clearance in the bladders of mice pre-treated with the NPs before infection (Fig. S6C). H&E staining images revealed significant immune cellular infiltration and edema in the bladders of control mice compared with those of NPs-treated mice (Fig. 5E). Importantly, treatment with the NPs alone did not elicit any adverse effects on bladders or other organs (Fig. 5E and Fig. S6D), and repeated administration of the NPs within a week did not result in any significant changes in body weight (Fig. S6E). Reduced CD45-positive cells, neutrophils, macrophages, and NK cells were observed in the bladders of the NPs-treated mice, in line with decreased UPEC and inflammation (Fig. 5F and Figs. S7A–B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of NPs on signaling pathways in the mouse model of acute UTI
ce:section.ce:para: Gene expression profiles of bladders from CFT073-infected mice following treatment with D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs, or the control group were analyzed using RNA-sequencing. In the bladders of the D-xyl@ɛPLCDs group, there were 3582 down-regulated genes and 598 up-regulated genes were compared to the control group (Fig. 6 A). The KEGG pathway analysis further revealed a significant enrichment of down-regulated genes in various inflammation signaling pathways, including PI3K-Akt, TNF, MAPK and NF-kappa B when comparing the D-xyl@εPLCDs group with the control group (Fig. 6B), and representative genes in these pathways were found down-regulated in the D-xyl@εPLCDs group (Fig. 6C–D, Figs. S7C–D). These results indicate a subsequent reduction in inflammation and tissue damage within the bladder following the clearance of bacterial infection by D-xyl@εPLCDs.

Due to its outstanding biosafety performance, biodegradability, and broad-spectrum bactericidal properties, we employed εPL in the synthesis of carbon dots with trackable fluorescence and D-xylose modification for studying intracellular clearance of UPEC. Different from those reported excitation wavelength-dependent blue fluorescent ɛPL-based CDs [9,40], the as-prepared green fluorescent ɛPLCDs here could provide a more powerful tool for the subsequent in vivo and in vitro studies. The conjugation of D-xylose on ɛPLCDs was intended to improve the intracellular bacterial targeting in infected cells and animals, thereby addressing the clinical challenge of recurrent UTI.

In vitro experiments have demonstrated that D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibit slightly superior bactericidal activity against ɛPLCDs. SEM and TEM images of UPEC treated by D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibited classic outer and inner cell membrane damage, membrane permeability alteration raised by cationic antimicrobial materials [41,42]. In contrast to ɛPL which exhibits very low absorption in the gastrointestinal tract in an acute oral animal model, the engineered NPs in our study showed a clear and significant uptake in epithelial cells. In the complex cellular microenvironment, the targeting of D-xylose significantly enhanced the efficacy of D-xyl@εPLCDs in eradicating intracellular bacteria, as demonstrated by multiple lines of evidence. The colocalization between CFT073 and D-xyl@εPLCDs was increased compared with ɛPLCDs, while the localization of CFT073 mutant strain ΔyadC with D-xyl@εPLCDs was reduced. Additionally, D-xyl@εPLCDs exhibited superior intracellular antibacterial activity against clinical bacterial strains in multiple bladder epithelial cell lines and acute UTI mouse models. Therefore, we have developed traceable and effective nanoparticles that target intracellular UPEC.

In contrast to the simplistic anti-adhesion approach, using sugar antagonists [18,43–45] such as glycosides, sugar-based polymers or NPs, D-xyl@εPLCDs offer a superior and synergistic treatment for UPEC infection in multiple steps. We have also implemented a novel approach utilizing εPL as the core of carbon dots, which exhibits superior biocompatibility and water solubility, distinct from other recently reported εPL-modified nanoparticles [9,10]. The use of non-antibiotic peptides with D-xylose modification in ɛPLCDs would also enhance the therapeutic effect and avoid the drug resistance and side effects of broad-spectrum antibiotics (Figs. S2E and S2F).

The present study aimed to evaluate the efficacy of engineered nanoparticles with intravesical therapeutic methods for treating urinary tract infections (UTIs), which are previously utilized in research settings [18,46,47]. Although oral or intravenous administration of antibiotics is commonly used in clinical settings for treating UTIs due to their convenience and applicability, the use of nanoparticles may not be suitable due to concerns about their biosafety. In fact, the application of nanoparticles within blood and alimentary tract has been associated with significant hepatic or intestinal injuries, as evidenced by numerous reports [48,49]. Since acute UTIs are typically confined to the urinary tract system, transurethral injection appears to be a rational choice due to its lower risk of systemic side effects (particularly in patients with renal and liver failure) and higher local drug concentration (which is more effective against multi-drug resistant bacterial strains) [50]. Furthermore, our animal data demonstrated their significant bactericidal activity in urine, which is a hostile environment (Fig. 6D). In addition, our previous study showed that administering 100 mg/kg D-xylose orally before or after the UTI reduced bacterial titers in mice bladders (Fig. 3e and f in Ref. [26]), supporting the potential p.o. administration method of NPs in this study.

Although the NPs in this study are mainly administered through the urethra route, there is still a possibility that they may indirectly accumulate in and affect other organs via blood circulation or other routes [51]. The histological changes of major organs were typically examined to monitor systemic reactions for the safety of the NPs in vivo [16,52–54]. Injuries of major organs, such as cardiovascular system [55], liver [48], spleen [56], lung [57], kidney [58] have been monitored during treatments of bacterial infection or nanoparticle therapies.

The analysis of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in NPs-treated 5637 cells indicated that the treatment of the NPs alone raised the activity of innate immune responses, such as IL-1β and TNF-α for macrophage activation, as well as the analysis from KEGG signaling pathway (Fig. S3). However, genes involved in inflammation signal pathways were down-regulated in NPs-treated mice with UTI (Fig. 6). These results indicate that the NPs promote innate immunity to clear infected intracellular bacteria, while restrict UPEC infection and improve organic status against inflammation caused by UTI. The significant changes in DEGs and KEGG pathways observed in cells and mice may provide valuable targets and guidelines for clinical therapeutics using nanomaterials [59,60].

In summary, the engineered NPs in this study have several advantages including: 1. The NPs retained their antibacterial activity in vitro while also exhibiting traceable properties. 2. The NPs exhibit superior performance in entering infected epithelial cells and have stronger bactericidal activity against intracellular UPEC, in comparison to the parental ε-poly-L-lysine which is host membrane-impermeable. 3. They have targeting ability to UPEC in cellular compartment due to its D-xylose modification; 4. The NPs failed to induce the development of resistant UPEC, in comparison to conventional antibiotics such as Gentamycin and Norfloxacin; 5. The NPs entering human bladder epithelial cells induce the generation of ROS, which synergistically enhances the antibacterial effect of εPL itself, providing multiple strategies to eliminate UPEC invasion [22,61].

The mechanisms by which D-xyl@εPLCDs inhibit UPEC infection are systematically elucidated from several perspectives (Scheme 1). First, in the extracellular lumen, the direct attachment of NPs to UPEC facilitates its bactericidal effect as reported in εPL studies, including the disrupted membrane, abnormal cytoplasmic distribution, and changes in membrane permeability. Secondly, the attachment of D-xyl@εPLCDs to YadC adhesin in UPEC impedes subsequent binding with receptors on infected epithelial cells, resulting in a reduced number of invaded bacteria present in the intracellular space. Thirdly, the D-xyl@εPLCDs are taken up by infected cells, enabling them to detect invaded UPEC in various cellular compartments such as the cytoplasm, vesicular compartment, and lysosomes through their D-xylose group and subsequently eliminate them. On the other hand, uptake of NPs induces ROS production within cellular compartments which is detrimental to invaded UPEC. The RNA sequencing analysis has confirmed an upregulation of immune response-related gene expression for bacteria defense in epithelial cells with D-xyl@εPLCDs treatment. Having excellent biosafety properties, in the mouse model of acute UTIs, D-xyl@εPLCDs demonstrated an overall effect on UPEC clearance and reduced inflammation in bladder tissue. These findings are further supported by flow cytometry and RNA sequencing analysis.

In summary, we have demonstrated that the engineered D-xyl@εPLCDs are safe, traceable and efficacious nanoparticles against UPEC infection both in vitro and in vivo. The D-xylose modification of these nanoparticles has greatly enhanced their bactericidal activity of εPL within intracellular spaces, providing a potent candidate for clinical drug development.

The reagents used in this study were as follows (Table S1): ε-poly-L-lysine (εPL, Mw∼4000, 28211-04-3), Acid Red 94 (AR94, 28211-04-3), Rhodamine B (81-88-9), D-xylose (58-86-6), MgSO4 (7487-88-9) and Glucose (50-99-7), D-glucose, Ninhydrin were purchased from HEOWNS (Xiensi Chemical Technology Co., LTD, Tianjin, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 16940-66-2) was obtained from Kermel (Kermel Chemical Technology Co., LTD). KCl (7447-40-7), CaCl2 (1305-62-0) and NaHCO3 (144-55-8) were purchased from Guangfu (Guangfu Technology Development Co., LTD, Tianjin, China). Hematoxylin (BA4041) and Eosin (BA4024) were purchased from BASO. RPMI1640 (L220KJ) and Trypsin (S310JV) were obtained from BasalMedia (Shanghai BasalMedia Technologies Co., LTD, Shanghai, China). PBS (BL302A) was purchased from Biosharp. 1% penicillin (AC03L332) and 1% streptomycin (AC03L332) were obtained from Life iLab (Shanghai Life iLab Biotech Co., LTD, Shanghai, China). DMEM (C11995500BT) was obtained from Gibco. FM4-64 (HY-103466) was purchased from MCE. DAPI (P0131), Lyso-Tracker (C1046) and DCFH-DA (S0033S) were purchased from Beyotime. Glutaraldehyde (G916054) was purchased from MACKLIN. Gentamycin sulfate (A620217-0005) and Bovine serum albumin (A600332) were purchased from BBI. Norfloxacin (N8550), 4% paraformaldehyde fixative solution (P1110), Triton X-100 (T8200), Normal goat serum (SL038), DNaseI (D8071) and agar powder (A8190) were purchased from Solarbio. Tryptone (LP0042) and yeast extract (LP0021) were purchased from OXOID. Cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB, 11865) was obtained from QDRS BIOTEC. Anti-LPS antibody (Ab35654) was purchased from Abcam. Tissue-Tek O.C.T. Compound (4583) was purchased from SAKURA. Collagenase from Clostridium histolyticum (C0130) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. TRIzol Reagent (15596018) was purchased from Thermo Fisher.

The morphology and particle size of the as-prepared carbon dots were studied by transmission electronic microscopy (The Thermo Scientific Talos F200C) and dynamic light scattering (DLS, BT-90, BETETER, China). FT-IR spectra were taken on a FT-IR spectrometer (Nexus870, Nicolet, America) using KBr pellets at room temperature. The composition of the prepared nanoparticles was obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were analyzed on an X-ray diffractometer (D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AVANCE 600 (Germany) in D2O. The photoluminescence (PL) was performed on a Fluorescence spectrometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis spectra were performed on a spectrophotometer (UV-5100, Metash, China).

The ε-poly-L-lysine carbon dots (denoted as ɛPLCDs) were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method according to a modified literature procedure [62]. First, εPL (500 mg, 4 mL) was mixed with AR94 (15 mg, 3 mL), subsequently the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-equipped static autoclave (25 mL) under the condition of 160°C for 4 h in an oven. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the reaction solution was centrifuged at12000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles, and dialyzed (MWCO, 3500 Da) for 72 h to remove the residual regents. Finally, the ɛPLCDs were collected by freeze-drying and stored at 4°C at dark for future use. To further fabricate D-xylose-modified ɛPLCDs, various mass percentages of D-xylose (10%, 20% and 40%) were added into the as-prepared ɛPLCDs solution (7 mL, 17 mg/mL). The reaction was continuously under 70°C for 6 h, and after cooling down to room temperature, followed by reacting overnight in the presence of NaBH4 (NaBH4/D-xylose molar ratio is 10) to obtain the stable D-xylose-modified ɛPLCDs (denoted as D-xyl10%@ɛPLCDs, D-xyl20%@ɛPLCDs and D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs). The reaction solution was dialyzed (MWCO, 1000 Da) to remove the residual regents. The final D-xyl@εPLCDs were collected by freeze-drying and stored in the dark at 4°C for future use [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relative fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
Rhodamine B (0.5 μg/mL, in ethanol, FLQY = 89%, excitation wavelength = 495 nm) was used as a standard reagent. The FLQY was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ =
A R
/ A × I /
I R
× η /
η R
×
Φ R
where Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the UV–vis absorbance at the fluorescence excitation wavelength, I denotes the integrated area in the emission spectrum, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of amines on ɛPLCDs surface
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 mL of ɛPLCDs aqueous solutions was first placed in test tubes, after adding 1.0 mL solution of ninhydrin in acetone (8%), the tubes were heated at boiled water for 5 min. The mixture was cooled to room temperature, then 1.0 mL ethanol (60%) was added. After being vortexed, and the absorbance value was measured at 570 nm using a multi-modal plate reader. Glycine with known concentrations was used as a standard to generate a standard curve.

The fluorescence intensity of D-xyl@εPLCDs under different conditions was recorded, e.g., the D-xyl@εPLCDs (60 μg/mL, 3 mL) were exposed to continuous excitation at 510 nm for 60 min, as well as in a series of solutions with different pH values (pH 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4), and PBS containing different intracellular species, including MgSO4 (2 mM), KCl (150 mM), NaHCO3 (10 mM), CaCl2 (2 mM), glucose (20 mM), and BSA (1 μM).

Human bladder epithelial cell lines 5637 (ATCC, HTB-9), J82 (ATCC, HTB-1) and T24 (ATCC, HTB-4) were cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 (RPMI1640, BasalMedia) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biological Industries) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (AC03L332, Life iLab) at 37°C with 5% CO2. Mouse macrophage cell line RAW 264.7 (ATCC, TIB-71), HK-2 (normal adult human kidney, ATCC, CRL-2190) and 2BS (normal human embryonic lung fibroblast) cells was cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco) with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C under a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2.

Uropathogenic Escherichia coli strains CFT073 (ATCC, 700928), UTI89 (a gift from Professor Harry L. T. Mobley) and clinical strains of UPEC (obtained from the Second Hospital of Tianjin Medical University) were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37°C until reaching the log growth phase with an OD600 of 0.6–1.0 prior to use. The ΔyadC strains of CFT073 and UTI89 were previously generated by substitution of yadC gene with a cat gene using the lambda red recombination system [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial targeting and extracellular antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: To determine bacterial growth curves [26], 105 Colony-forming units (CFU) of pre-cultured CFT073 were incubated with εPLCDs materials in 200 μL LB medium in 96-well plates at 37°C. The optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured every hour for a total of 12 h. The OD600 value of the LB medium was set as background.

For in vitro bacterial targeting, 108 CFU of pre-cultured CFT073 or ΔyadC strains were incubated with D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL in 500 μL PBS for 30 min at 37°C and 180 rpm. After being washed with PBS, the bacteria were stained with 20 μg/mL FM4-64 (HY-103466, MCE) on ice for 30 min and then mounted onto glass slides using Antifade Mounting Medium with DAPI (P0131, Beyotime). Images of stained bacteria were taken using a confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica TCS-SP8 DMi8, Leica Microsystems, Germany). Intensity profile analysis was carried out by using Image Pro Plus software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: For SEM analysis of NPs treated bacteria, 109 CFU of pre-cultured CFT073 were incubated with the NPs of 200 μg/mL in 500 μL PBS for 2 h at 37°C, 180 rpm. The bacterial mixture was centrifuged and washed with PBS to remove unbound NPs, followed by fixation of 2.5% glutaraldehyde in H2O (G916054, MACKLIN) at 4°C for 4 h. The fixed bacteria were then dehydrated by series of alcohol solutions (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%) for 10 min. Finally, the fixed bacteria were completely dehydrated using pure alcohol for 30 min three times, and then frozen at −80°C. The SEM samples were then prepared according to the manufacturer's standard instructions. SEM (SUPRA55, Carl Zeiss, Germany) was used to study the morphologies of bacteria. For TEM visualization of CFT073 from infected cells, the infected cells after NPs treatment were trypsinized and centrifuged. The pellets were then fixed in a 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution at 4°C overnight and processed according to standard procedures for TEM analysis (Tecnai G2 Spirit, FEI; HT7700, HITACHI), as previously described [64].

The LIVE/DEAD Fixable Near-IR Dead Cell Stain Kit (L34976) was utilized to perform the live and dead viability/cytotoxicity assay in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. 108 CFU of pre-cultured CFT073 were incubated with D-xyl40%@ɛPLCDs at a concentration of 200 μg/mL in 500 μL PBS for 30 min at 37°C and 180 rpm. After being washed with PBS, the treated CFT073 was resuspended in 1 mL of PBS and mixed with a staining solution consisting of 1 μL NucGreen, 2 μL EthD-III, and 8 μL PBS. Following a 15-min incubation at room temperature, the samples were visualized using a fluorescent confocal microscope.

To determine the membrane permeability, 108 CFU of pre-cultured CFT073 were incubated with the NPs at a concentration of 200 μg/mL in 500 μL PBS for 24 h at 37°C and 180 rpm. The mixture was incubated with 20 μL of TO-PRO-3 solution (R37170, Invitrogen) for 30 min at room temperature under light-free conditions, fixed by 4% formaldehyde, and subjected to flow cytometry analysis following the instruction from the manufacturer.

To determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of NPs against CFT073, 104 CFU of logarithmic-phase CFT073 were cultured in a 96-well plate containing 100 μL of cation-adjusted Mueller-Hinton broth (CAMHB) with serially diluted concentrations of NPs. After incubation for 24 h, the MIC was determined as the lowest drug concentration that significantly inhibited microbial growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro development of resistant strains
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment was carried out with a modified method as previously reported [65]. To assess bacterial resistance development to the NPs, CFT073 was cultured in CAMHB with increasing concentrations of the NPs, Gentamycin, and Norfloxacin at 0.25-, 0.5-, 1-, 2-, and 4-fold MIC. After a 24-h incubation period, the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured for each culture. The strain with an OD600 of 1 in the highest drug concentration was then washed and grown in fresh CAMHB media with increasing concentrations of the drug at fold increments of 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 for next generation, meanwhile the MIC of the strain was measured and recorded. The passage was repeated for 10 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular bacteria targeting and antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: For the bacterial invasion assay, human bladder 5637 cells were pre-cultured in 24-well plates for 24 h. Bacteria strains in log-phase were added into the culture medium at an MOI of 100, followed by centrifugation of 500×g for 5 min. After 1 h of incubation, infected cells were washed with PBS three times, and treated with 100 μg/mL Gentamycin plus NPs at proper concentrations for about 3 h. Infected cells were then washed and lysed using 0.2% Triton X-100 for 30 min at room temperature. CFU of bacteria in cell lysate were counted after plating on LB agar.

To assess bacterial entry, 5637 cells were infected by CFT073 at an MOI of 100 for half an hour to facilitate adherence and initial internalization of CFT073 into the cells. The infected cells were washed and subsequently treated with the NPs and Gentamycin for another half an hour to eliminate extracellular UPEC, and allow the interaction between the NPs and UPEC which is being-internalized, without providing significant replication time for UPEC. Then, the infected cells were washed again and lysed for CFU measurement of entry. To assess the overall impact of the NPs on intracellular and extracellular bacteria, CFT073-infected 5637 cells were treated with the NPs in the absence of gentamycin for 1 h, washed and incubated for additional period of time. Both supernatant and lysate from infected cells were collected for CFU quantification.

For the bacterial adhesion assay, 5637 cells were pre-treated with the NPs of 200 μg/mL and centrifuged at 500×g for 5 min. After 1 h of incubation, 5637 cells were washed with PBS and infected with bacteria strains in log-phase at an MOI of 100 for 1 h. Infected 5637 cells were then washed with PBS three times, digested by trypsin and plated on LB agar for CFU measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake of the NPs and intracellular colocalization with the lysosome within cells
ce:section.ce:para: 5637 cells grown in a 4-well chamber were treated with 100 μg/mL NPs for different periods of time (0.5, 1 and 3 h), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and stained with DAPI solution. For staining of lysosomes, 500 μL of pre-warmed Lyso-Tracker Red working solution (C1046, Beyotime) were added to live cells for 1 h before fixation. The Pearson's coefficient value was calculated by Image-Pro Plus software (Media Cybernetics).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colocalization of intracellular bacteria and the NPs
ce:section.ce:para: For detecting the colocalization of intracellular bacteria and the NPs, infected 5637 cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and permeabilized by 0.1% Triton X-100. Infected cells were then blocked with 5% BSA/PBS containing goat serum (SL038, Solarbio) for 30 min, and stained with an anti-LPS antibody for 1 h at room temperature. Cells were then washed with PBS and further stained with a CoraLite594-conjugated Goat Anti-Mouse IgG (H + L) secondary antibody for 1 h. The cell nuclei were stained with DAPI solutions. Confocal images were taken by a fluorescent microscope (Leica TCS-SP8 DMi8, Leica Microsystems, Germany).

To measure the ROS production in mammalian cells, 5637 and RAW 264.7 cells were pre-cultured in 12-well plates for 24 h 5637 Cells were then treated with or without 200 μg/mL NPs for 3 h, and incubated with 20 μM DCFH-DA at 37°C for the last 90 min. In the case of RAW 264.7 cells, they were treated with NPs and 20 μM DCFH-DA for 9 h. Cells were digested with trypsin and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde. Fixed cells were filtered with a 0.2 μm filter and subjected to analysis by flow cytometry.

1 × 104 of 5637 cells were seeded in 96-well plates for 24 h and treated with 200 μL culture medium containing varying concentrations of the NPs (1, 5, 25, 100, 200 μg/mL). After 24 or 48-h incubation, Cell Counting Kit-8 (C6005S, UElandy) was added to measure the cell viability according to the manufacturer's method.

Female C57BL/6J mice (obtained from the Academy of Military Medical Science, Beijing, China) were maintained in regular specific pathogen free (SPF) conditions and used with approvals of the Animal Ethics Committee at Tianjin Medical University according to the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (TMUaMEC2021040).

For the mouse model of acute UTI 6- to 8-week-old female mice were anesthetized and transurethrally injected with 108 CFU of log-phase growing CFT073 in 50 μL sterilized PBS. For determining the bacterial clearance effects of the NPs, infected mice were transurethrally injected again with 100 μL NPs of 200 μg/mL at 6 h post infection (hpi) and maintained overnight. For acute UTI prevention by the NPs, female mice were first transurethrally injected with 100 μL NPs of 200 μg/mL and then incubated with CFT073 at 6 hpi. Mice were then euthanized for the bladder collection. The mouse bladders were aseptically harvested and homogenized with 0.025% Triton X-100. The CFU of the bladder was determined by plating the tissue homogenate on LB agar.

To monitor the in vivo distribution of the NPs, the major organs and tissues (including Heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and bladder) were collected from mice at 12 h post transurethrally injection with 100 μL NPs of 200 μg/mL. Fluorescence images of mice were taken by IVIS spectrum in vivo imaging system (IVIS, PerkinElmer, America).

For immunofluorescence staining, mouse bladders embedded in OCT compound were frozen in liquid nitrogen and made for sections on glass slides. After fixation with acetone, frozen sections were treated with cold methanol and 3% hydrogen peroxide, and then rehydrated. Blockage of sections was performed with 5% BSA/PBS at room temperature for 1 h, followed by incubation of primary antibody overnight at 4°C. After three times washes, sections were stained using CoraLite 488- or 592-conjugated secondary antibodies plus DAPI. Confocal fluorescence images were taken using a fluorescence microscope (Leica TCS-SP8 DMi8, Leica Microsystems, Germany).

Female C57BL/6J mice were randomly separated into 3 groups (n = 6) and transurethrally injected with 100 μL NPs of 200 μg/mL, or H2O once every two days for 1 week. The weigh of mice was recorded every day. On the 7th Day, the main organs and tissues including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and bladder were collected and stained with the H&E assay for physiological evaluation.

Mouse tissues were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde, embedded in paraffin, and cut into 5 μm sections. Tissue sections were further stained with hematoxylin and eosin. Images were taken using an Olympus microscope (Tokyo, Japan).

For tissue cell staining, the mouse bladder was collected and processed into single-cell suspensions using the following steps: The tissue was thoroughly minced and digested with 500 μL of PBS containing type IV collagenase (20 μg/mL) and DNaseI (10 μg/mL) for 40 min at 37°C with constant shaking at 180 rpm. The resulting cell mixture was washed with PBS, homogenized, and filtered to obtain a single-cell suspension. The single-cell suspensions were stained at 4°C in the dark with antibodies as follows: anti-CD45 conjugated to PerCP-Cyanine5.5 (Biolegend, 1003132); anti-CD11b conjugated to PE-Cyanine7 (Biolegend, 101216), anti-F4/80 conjugated to FITC (Biolegend, 123108), anti-Ly6G conjugated to APC (Invitrogen, 17-9668-82), anti-CD45 conjugated to PE-Cyanine7 (Biolegend, 103113), anti-CD3 conjugated to PE (Biolegend, 100205), anti-NK1.1 conjugated to PerCP-Cyanine5.5 (Biolegend, 108728). Data was acquired using the FACS Canto II Flow Cytometer (BD Biosciences, Becton, Dickinson and Company, America) and subsequently analyzed with the FlowJo software (FlowJo, Ashland, OR, America).

For cell RNA sequencing, 5637 cells were exposed to the NPs at a concentration of 200 μg/mL for 3 h. The cells were subsequently trypsinized, rinsed with PBS and mixed with TRIzol. For tissue RNA sequencing, mice were infected with CFT073 for 6 h and then treated with the NPs overnight. The bladder tissue of mice infected with CFT073 was collected after the NPs treatment, sterilized with 75% ethanol, and then frozen in liquid nitrogen after being rinsed with PBS. RNA extraction was performed using the standard TRIzol method according to the manufacturer's instructions (Invitrogen). The data on differential gene expression, KEGG pathway, and heatmap of target enriched genes were analyzed using the Majorbio Cloud Platform online (www.majorbio.com). The referenced data have been deposited in NCBI's Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) with accession number GSE236506 and GSE236507.
All results were shown as the mean ± SD and analyzed for statistical significance using Student's t-test or ANOVA analysis.
